case_name: New India Assurance Co. Ltd. vs Yallavva W/o Yamanappa Dharanakeri
court: High Court of Karnataka at Bengaluru
compensation_amount: Rs.1,000/- (plus interest at 6% p.a. from date of petition until deposit)

compensation_breakdown.tribunal_amount: Rs.1,000/- along with interest at 6% p.a. from the date of petition till the date of deposit of compensation
compensation_breakdown.final_amount: Rs.1,000/- (plus interest at 6% p.a. from date of petition until deposit)
compensation_breakdown.interest: 6% p.a. from date of petition till deposit
compensation_breakdown.notes: Tribunal award of Rs.1,000/- upheld; insurer directed to pay and entitled to recover from insured owner.

facts: On 24.04.2006 at about 11:00 AM a Tempo 407 (Reg. No. KA-28/B-5434), a goods vehicle, turned turtle near Horti village while plying from Tikota to Dhulkhed carrying coolies. Claimant Yallavva sustained grievous injuries and filed a claim before MACT-V Bijapur. The Tribunal allowed the petition partly and awarded Rs.1,000/- with interest, directing New India Assurance Company to pay and recover from the vehicle owner. The insurer appealed alleging breach of policy (gratuitous passengers in a goods vehicle) and sought to be discharged of liability.
arguments_claimant: The claimant contended she sustained injuries due to rash and negligent driving of the tempo and was entitled to compensation. The existence and currency of the insurance policy was not disputed at the claim stage and the Tribunal awarded Rs.1,000/- with interest, directing the insurer to pay.
arguments_respondent: The insurer argued there was a breach of the policy condition because the claimant was a gratuitous passenger in a goods vehicle and therefore the insurer had no liability under Section 149(2). The insurer relied on earlier Division Bench authority (Oriental Insurance Co. v. K.C. Subramanyam) and argued that where a statutory defence under Section 149(2) is proved the insurer must be absolved and cannot be directed to 'pay and recover' except in rare circumstances or by Supreme Court exercising Article 142.
ratio_decidendi: "If the vehicle involved in an accident is duly insured and a certificate of insurance is provided as per Section 147 of the Act, it is a statutory policy, the Insurance Company is absolutely liable to pay compensation." The Court held that "The Insurer is liable to pay the third party and recover from the insured even if there is breach of any condition recognized under Section 149(2), even if it is a fundamental breach (that is breach of condition which is the cause for the accident) and the insurer proves the said breach, in view of the mandate under Section 149(1) of the Act." The Court qualified this by stating that "no such order can be passed against the insurer, if, on the facts and circumstances of a case, a finding is given by the court that the third party (injured or deceased) had played any fraud or was in collusion with the insured." Finally the Court explained that this rule applies whether the policy is a statutory 'Act' policy or a contractual policy (where extra premium was taken) and that the insurer, even if it proves a defence under Section 149(2)(b) (policy void for non-disclosure), "is not absolved of its liability to satisfy the judgment or award" and may recover from the insured afterwards.
final_order: The appeal is dismissed. The Tribunal's award of Rs.1,000/- with interest at 6% p.a. is sustained; the appellant insurer was directed to discharge the liability (and is entitled to recover the amount from the insured owner); parties to bear their respective costs.
summary: This is an appeal by New India Assurance against a MACT award of Rs.1,000/- (plus interest) to an injured claimant who was travelling as a gratuitous passenger in a goods tempo that overturned. The insurer argued breach of policy (gratuitous passengers) under Section 149(2) and sought to be absolved. The High Court held that even if the insurer proves a statutory defence or the policy is contractual/void, Section 149(1) permits a 'pay and recover' direction — the insurer must satisfy the award to protect third parties and may recover from the insured, except where the third party colluded to cause the breach. Appeal dismissed and award upheld.
embedding_summary: New India Assurance Co. Ltd. v. Yallavva W/o Yamanappa Dharanakeri, High Court of Karnataka, 12 May 2020 (AIRONLINE 2020 KAR 986; M.F.A. No.30131/2010). Accident on 24.04.2006 near Horti village: tempo 407 (KA-28/B-5434) goods vehicle turned turtle; claimant injured. Key statutes: Sections 146, 147, 149 (esp. 149(2)(a) and 149(2)(b)), 156, 163A, 165, 168, 174 Motor Vehicles Act 1988; Article 142 Constitution invoked in precedents. Core issue: whether insurer can be directed to 'pay and recover' when policy conditions (gratuitous passenger/other breaches) fall under Section 149(2) or when policy is contractual/void. Court applied the Swaran Singh twin tests (rule of main purpose and fundamental breach) but held insurer must nonetheless satisfy award to protect third parties and may recover from insured; exception where third party colluded/fraud. Outcome: appeal dismissed; award of Rs.1,000/- plus 6% p.a. interest upheld; insurer to discharge award and entitled to recover from owner. Precedents relied on include Swaran Singh, Baljit Kaur, C.M. Jaya, Challa Bharathamma, Brij Mohan, Shamanna, Pappu, and several others.
legal_principles: Pay-and-recover doctrine under Section 149(1)/149(4): insurer must satisfy third-party award and may recover from insured
Swaran Singh twin tests: insurer must prove breach and that breach is a 'fundamental breach' affecting cause of accident (main-purpose rule)
Statutory protection for third parties: compulsory 'Act' coverage under Sections 146–147 cannot be defeated by policy terms except as in Section 149(2)
Policy voidability (Section 149(2)(b)) does not absolve insurer from initial obligation to satisfy award; insurer may recover from insured
Exception for collusion/fraud: courts may refuse pay-and-recover where third party colluded with insured to cause breach
Contractual enlargement of liability: where extra premium is taken, contractual liability may increase insurer's obligations but pay-and-recover still applies
cases_cited: National Insurance Co. Ltd. vs Swaran Singh, (2004) 3 SCC 297
New India Assurance Co. Ltd. vs Baljit Kaur, (2004) 2 SCC 1
New India Assurance Co. Ltd. vs C.M. Jaya, (2002) 2 SCC 278

Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd. vs Brij Mohan, (2007) 7 SCC 56
New India Insurance Co. vs Darshanadevi, (2008) 7 SCC 416
Samundradevi and Others vs Narendra Kaur and Others, (2008) 9 SCC 100
Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd. vs Nanjappan, (AIR 2004 SC 1630)
National Insurance Co. Ltd. vs Laxmi Narain Dhut, (2007) 3 SCC 700
National Insurance Co. Ltd. vs Kaushalaya Devi (Amrit Lal Sood case), (2008) 8 SCC 246
New India Assurance Co. Ltd. vs Parvathneni, (2009) 8 SCC 785
Balbir Kaur and Others vs New India Assurance Co. Ltd., (2009) 13 SCC 370 (ref'd)
Shamanna and another vs Divisional Manager, Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd., (2018) 9 SCC 650
Pappu and Others vs Vinod Kumar Lamba and another, (AIR 2018 SC 592)
Singh Ram and Nirmala and Others (2018 ACJ 1264)
Rani and Others vs National Insurance Co. Ltd. and Others (2018 ACJ 2430)
Anu Bhanavara etc. vs Iffco Tokio General Insurance Co. Ltd. and Others (Civil Appeals 6231-6232 of 2019)
United India Insurance Co. Ltd. vs Jaimy, (1998 ACJ 1318) (Kerala)
bm25_text: New India Assurance Co. Ltd. vs Yallavva W/o Yamanappa Dharanakeri High Court of Karnataka at Bengaluru 2020 On 24.04.2006 at about 11:00 AM a Tempo 407 (Reg. No. KA-28/B-5434), a goods vehicle, turned turtle near Horti village while plying from Tikota to Dhulkhed carrying coolies. Claimant Yallavva sustained grievous injuries and filed a claim before MACT-V Bijapur. The Tribunal allowed the petition partly and awarded Rs.1,000/- with interest, directing New India Assurance Company to pay and recover from the vehicle owner. The insurer appealed alleging breach of policy (gratuitous passengers in a goods vehicle) and sought to be discharged of liability. The claimant contended she sustained injuries due to rash and negligent driving of the tempo and was entitled to compensation. The existence and currency of the insurance policy was not disputed at the claim stage and the Tribunal awarded Rs.1,000/- with interest, directing the insurer to pay. The insurer argued there was a breach of the policy condition because the claimant was a gratuitous passenger in a goods vehicle and therefore the insurer had no liability under Section 149(2). The insurer relied on earlier Division Bench authority (Oriental Insurance Co. v. K.C. Subramanyam) and argued that where a statutory defence under Section 149(2) is proved the insurer must be absolved and cannot be directed to 'pay and recover' except in rare circumstances or by Supreme Court exercising Article 142. "If the vehicle involved in an accident is duly insured and a certificate of insurance is provided as per Section 147 of the Act, it is a statutory policy, the Insurance Company is absolutely liable to pay compensation." The Court held that "The Insurer is liable to pay the third party and recover from the insured even if there is breach of any condition recognized under Section 149(2), even if it is a fundamental breach (that is breach of condition which is the cause for the accident) and the insurer proves the said breach, in view of the mandate under Section 149(1) of the Act." The Court qualified this by stating that "no such order can be passed against the insurer, if, on the facts and circumstances of a case, a finding is given by the court that the third party (injured or deceased) had played any fraud or was in collusion with the insured." Finally the Court explained that this rule applies whether the policy is a statutory 'Act' policy or a contractual policy (where extra premium was taken) and that the insurer, even if it proves a defence under Section 149(2)(b) (policy void for non-disclosure), "is not absolved of its liability to satisfy the judgment or award" and may recover from the insured afterwards. The appeal is dismissed. The Tribunal's award of Rs.1,000/- with interest at 6% p.a. is sustained; the appellant insurer was directed to discharge the liability (and is entitled to recover the amount from the insured owner); parties to bear their respective costs. This is an appeal by New India Assurance against a MACT award of Rs.1,000/- (plus interest) to an injured claimant who was travelling as a gratuitous passenger in a goods tempo that overturned. The insurer argued breach of policy (gratuitous passengers) under Section 149(2) and sought to be absolved. The High Court held that even if the insurer proves a statutory defence or the policy is contractual/void, Section 149(1) permits a 'pay and recover' direction — the insurer must satisfy the award to protect third parties and may recover from the insured, except where the third party colluded to cause the breach. Appeal dismissed and award upheld. New India Assurance Co. Ltd. v. Yallavva W/o Yamanappa Dharanakeri, High Court of Karnataka, 12 May 2020 (AIRONLINE 2020 KAR 986; M.F.A. No.30131/2010). Accident on 24.04.2006 near Horti village: tempo 407 (KA-28/B-5434) goods vehicle turned turtle; claimant injured. Key statutes: Sections 146, 147, 149 (esp. 149(2)(a) and 149(2)(b)), 156, 163A, 165, 168, 174 Motor Vehicles Act 1988; Article 142 Constitution invoked in precedents. Core issue: whether insurer can be directed to 'pay and recover' when policy conditions (gratuitous passenger/other breaches) fall under Section 149(2) or when policy is contractual/void. Court applied the Swaran Singh twin tests (rule of main purpose and fundamental breach) but held insurer must nonetheless satisfy award to protect third parties and may recover from insured; exception where third party colluded/fraud. Outcome: appeal dismissed; award of Rs.1,000/- plus 6% p.a. interest upheld; insurer to discharge award and entitled to recover from owner. Precedents relied on include Swaran Singh, Baljit Kaur, C.M. Jaya, Challa Bharathamma, Brij Mohan, Shamanna, Pappu, and several others.
